segments.s385-g1: سرانجام is an adverb meaning 'at last' placed before the subject; گشتاسپ is the subject; بنمود is a poetic past tense verb (بنمود = 'showed/turned') in 3rd person singular; پشت is the noun complement 'back' (here the body-part object: 'turned his back'). The verb follows the subject in typical Persian order and the possessive is implied on پشت (i.e. 'his back').
segments.s385-g2: بدانگه is a temporal adverb 'thereupon/at that moment'; که introduces the subordinate clause ('that'); شد is the past intransitive 'became'; روزگارش is 'his fate/fortune' with the possessive suffix -ش; درشت is a predicate adjective 'harsh/rough'. Word order places the verb شد before the predicate adjective, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s385-g3: پس is a temporal 'then'; اندر introduces the locative phrase 'in/through'; دو منزل uses منزل in the sense 'stages (of a journey)' — 'in two stages'; همی is the pre-verbal continuous/iterative particle common in poetry; تاختند is past 3rd-person-plural 'they rushed/galloped/charged.'
segments.s385-g4: مر here is an archaic clitic/dative form meaning 'to/for me' placed before the object phrase; او را is the definite object 'him' (او with the object marker را); گرفتن is the infinitive 'to capture/take' functioning as the object of ساختند; همی again marks iterative/ongoing action; ساختند is past 3rd-person-plural 'they made/arranged/contrived.' Together مر او را گرفتن همی ساختند = 'they contrived/arranged to seize him for me.'
segments.s385-g5: یکی is the indefinite 'a certain'; کوه is 'mountain'; پیش here functions as 'before/in the presence of' and combines with آمدش ('came to him') — آمدش is the past 'came' with the enclitic pronoun -ش 'to him'; پرگیا is a proper name identifying the mountain. Word order places the action 'came to him' after the noun phrase, as common in epic diction.
segments.s385-g6: بدو is the allative 'to it/unto it' (referring back to the mountain); اندرون is the locative 'inside/within'; چشمه 'spring' and آسیا 'mill' are simply coordinated by و. The clause omits a copula, so the nouns function as an existential list 'within it, a spring and a mill (were/are).'
segments.s385-g7: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'for/that'; بر گرد is the compound prepositional phrase 'around' (بر + گرد); آن کوه 'that mountain'; یک راه 'one path' (راه = 'path/road'); بود is the past copula 'there was.' The clause states existence: 'around that mountain there was one path.'
segments.s385-g8: وزان is a fused poetic form (و + از آن) meaning 'and from that/of that'; راه repeats 'path'; گشتاسپ is the subject; آگاه بود is a predicative adjective plus past copula 'was aware' — together 'Gushtasp was aware of that path.' Word order places the prepositional link before subject and predicate.
segments.s385-g49: گر introduces a conditional protasis ('if'). او را is the definite-object marked pronoun ('him' with را marking the object). ببینم is 1st-person present-subjunctive (used here for a future/conditional sense: 'if I see'). برین رزمگاه is a prepositional phrase: بر + ین (archaic/poetic spelling of این) + رزمگاه, literally 'on that battlefield.' Word order (object then verb in the clause) and the archaic spelling are typical of Ferdowsi's diction.
segments.s385-g50: بدو is an indirect-object form 'to him' placed before the verb (poetic order). بخشم is 1st-person present/subjunctive (apodosis of the conditional) meaning 'I will give/bestow.' این introduces the items that follow as definite ('this/these'). The coordinated list تاج و تخت و کلاه is the direct object (three items: crown, throne, and hat/helmet).
segments.s385-g51: که introduces a clause that explains or results from the previous offer ('that / so that'). یارد شدن is a deverbal nominal phrase 'to become (made) a yārd/companion' (شدن as the light verb 'become/make'). پیش آن ارجمند means 'in the presence of that esteemed one' (پیش = 'before/in the presence of', آن ارجمند = 'that noble/esteemed person').
segments.s385-g52: رهاند is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive used as future ('he will free/deliver'). مران is a proper name (the direct object), modified by بیگنه ('the stranger/alien' used as an epithet). را is the definite-object marker attaching to the noun phrase. ز بند is a prepositional phrase 'from the fetter/bond' (ز = 'from', بند = 'fetter, bond'). The verb precedes its object here as is common in poetic lines.
segments.s385-g53: بدو before گفت marks the indirect object ('to him'). گفت here is the past-tense verb 'said' (word-list senses given do not include this meaning, see note). جاماسپ is the speaker's name. کای شهریار is a vocative/title: کای as an address ('O') or royal title plus شهریار 'king' — 'O king.' The sequence is poetic inversion: indirect-object marker + verb + speaker + vocative.
segments.s385-g54: منم is emphatic 'I am' (first-person, here asserting the speaker). رفتنی is an adjective/participial sense 'about to go' or 'one who will go/leave' (شدن omitted; used predicatively). کاین is an archaic/poetic form ('that/this' or 'that which') introducing the following noun. سخن نیست خوار is a small clause: سخن = 'speech/words', نیست = 'is not', خوار = 'despicable/low'; word order places the predicate after the negator, yielding 'this speech is not contemptible.'
segments.s385-g55: به + [name] + گفت is the standard 'said to Jamasp' construction (به = 'to/for'). جاماسپ is the addressee. شاه جهاندار is an appositive/title for the speaker: شاه 'king' and جهاندار 'ruler/conqueror' (a royal epithet). The verb گفت is 'said' (again the listed senses do not include 'to say').
segments.s385-g56: که introduces the content of what was said. با تو همیشه خرد is the phrase 'with you always [be] wisdom' (با = 'with', تو = 'you', همیشه = 'always', خرد = 'wisdom'). باد is the optative particle 'may it be' (expressing a blessing/wish). جفت ('pair/companion') functions as a predicate/complement: 'may wisdom be a companion to you always.' Word order places the wished-upon noun before the optative particle, as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s385-g89: خرد یافته is a compound where خرد (literally 'wisdom' or 'the wise one') + یافته (past participle 'having found' / 'found') yields 'the wise (one), having acquired wisdom' — a fronted descriptive phrase. چون here functions as a classical conjunction 'when/as' introducing the time clause. بیامد is the simple past (3rd-person) 'he/it came', and به دشت is the prepositional phrase 'into/to the plain' with به marking direction/goal.
segments.s385-g90: شب تیره is a noun + adjective ('night' + 'dark') with the adjective after the noun as in classical Persian. از لشکر indicates separation or origin ('from the host/army'), اندر is an archaic/preclassical locative particle meaning 'in/among/within' and here helps locate the action relative to the army, and گذشت is the past verb 'passed' — together giving 'a dark night passed from/over the host' (i.e., night departed the army).
segments.s385-g91: چو آمد is the classical conjunction + past verb 'when he came' (چو = 'when' here). به نزد is a classical locative sequence meaning 'to/near' (به + نزد = 'to the presence/nearby of'), followed by دژ 'fortress'. گنبدان is a place-name or epithet ('Gonbadān' or 'the domed [fortress]') completing the noun phrase 'the fortress of Gonbadān'. Word order is typical classical Persian: temporal clause (چو آمد) + prepositional locative phrase + noun.
segments.s385-g92: رهانید is a past tense verb 'freed/released' (third-person past). خود را is a reflexive object construction: خود 'self' with the object marker را emphasizing the definite object ('himself'). ز دست is a classical genitive/ablative phrase 'from the hand/from the power of' (i.e., out of someone's control). The last two tokens (ب + دان) together form the classical fused demonstrative/particle 'بدان' meaning 'those' / 'to those' (i.e., 'from the hands of those [men]'); the input splits them but they function as one demonstrative here.
segments.s385-g41: بدو گفت گشتاسپ is an inverted word order common in poetry: بدو = 'to him' (indirect object) comes before گفت (said) and the speaker گشتاسپ follows the verb. کای is a vocative particle 'O', followed by the epithet راست‌گوی 'truthful' addressing the addressee. Note that گفت appears here as the simple past 'said' despite the senses list given.
segments.s385-g42: This clause is a nominal negation: بجز راستی نیست ایچ آرزوی literally 'except truth there is not any wish', i.e. 'there is no desire except truth.' ایچ is an archaic/poetic negator 'nothing/none', راستی is the noun 'truth', and نیست is the negative copula 'is not / does not exist.' Word order places the exception phrase بجز راستی first for emphasis.
segments.s385-g43: به جاماسپ گفت is 'he said to Jamasp': به marks the indirect object. The direct address follows: ای خردمند مرد = 'O wise man' (ای = vocative, خردمند = 'wise', مرد = 'man' used here as an honorific vocative noun). The verb گفت precedes the vocative phrase because of poetic inversion.
segments.s385-g44: مرا بود ازان کار دل پر ز درد: مرا بود is an idiomatic sequence meaning 'it was to me' or 'I had / I was' (object clitic + copula). ازان کار introduces the cause 'because of that affair'. دل پر ز درد is a nominal predicate 'the heart (دل) full (پر) of pain (درد)', with ز as the poetic preposition 'from/because of'. Overall: 'Because of that affair my heart was full of pain.'
segments.s385-g45: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'). اورا (object clitic + accusative) precedes the verb, a common poetic order; ببستم is a past tense verb here meaning 'I bound/closed/confined' (poetic variant of بستم). بران بزمگاه 'in/at that banquet-hall' (بران = 'at/on that', بزمگاه = 'banquet-hall') gives the place of the action.
segments.s385-g46: به گفتار بدخواه means 'by the speech/words of an ill-wisher' — به here is instrumental 'by' and گفتار بدخواه qualifies the means (malicious talk). The conjunction و links the following nominal comment او بیگناه 'and he (is) innocent' where the copula is omitted (a common poetic nominal clause): 'and he is innocent.'
segments.s385-g47: همانگاه = 'at once / immediately' (adverb). The subject من follows, then زان پشیمان شدم = 'I became remorseful because of that' (زان = 'from that / because of that', پشیمان = adjectival predicate 'regretful', شدم = past of شدن 'became'). Word order places the temporal adverb first for emphasis.
segments.s385-g48: دلم خسته بد سوی درمان شدم: دلم is 'my heart' (دل + enclitic -م = 'my'); خسته is a predicate participle 'weary'. بد سوی درمان شدم literally 'I became / turned (شدم) in an ill/bad direction toward (سوی) remedy (درمان)'; here بد qualifies the manner ('in a pained/ill state') and سوی + درمان indicates the direction/aim 'toward cure.' The second شدم repeats the copular/kinetic verb 'became/turned' used idiomatically for setting out.
segments.s385-g9: This is a nominal phrase listing the actor(s): جهاندار گشتاسپ (the ruler Gushtasp) followed by و یکسر سپاه (and the entire army). The order places the title before the proper name; یکسر is an adverbial adjective meaning 'whole/entire' modifying سپاه. No verb appears in this segment; it supplies the subjects for the following verb.
segments.s385-g10: سوی کوه is a prepositional/directional phrase 'toward the mountain' placed before the verb for emphasis. رفتند is the past tense, 3rd-person plural 'they went', agreeing with the compound subject in the previous segment. ز آوردگاه uses the poetic preposition ز (classical equivalent of از) meaning 'from' and names the origin 'the battlefield.'
segments.s385-g11: چو here is a subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. ارجاسپ is the subject; با لشکر 'with the army' is an accompanying prepositional phrase; آنجا is the locative 'there'; رسید is the past tense 3rd-person singular 'arrived.'
segments.s385-g12: بگردید here is a classical/epic past form meaning 'he turned/looked about' (an intransitive motion/look verb in past narrative). It is followed by و 'and', then بر کوه 'on/upon the mountain' and راهی ندید 'he saw no way/path' — ندید is past negative 'did not see.' Note the idiomatic sequence: he scanned/turned about and found there was no path up the mountain.
segments.s385-g13: گرفتند is past 3rd-person plural 'they took/seized' and here functions with the following phrase to mean 'they surrounded/encircled.' گرداندرش is a compound expressing the idea of 'round/about him' or 'his attendants/around him' in this context, and چار سوی 'on four sides' is an idiom meaning 'on every side.' The verb is plural, indicating multiple assailants doing the surrounding.
segments.s385-g14: چو again = 'when' introducing the subordinate clause. بیچاره شد = 'became wretched/helpless' (شد is past ‘became’). شاه آزاده‌خوی is the noun phrase 'the free‑spirited / magnanimous king' (epic epithet after the noun). The clause describes the king's change of state at that moment.
segments.s385-g15: ازان is the poetic contraction of از آن 'from/at that' (here functioning locatively: 'at that [mountain]'), and کوهسار 'mountainside' names the place. آتش افروختند is a compound verbal phrase: آتش (fire) + افروختند (they kindled), past 3rd-person plural.
segments.s385-g16: بدان is an archaic locative combination (بَ + آن) meaning roughly 'on/at that' and heads the phrase بَدَان خاره 'on those stones.' خاره (here 'stones/pebbles') followed by بر خار ('upon thorn[s]') gives a piled or repeated image; می‌سوختند is the imperfective past (they were burning / they burned repeatedly), describing ongoing burning of material on the rocks. The construction thus conveys 'on those stones, thorn upon thorn was burning.'
segments.s385-g33: بدو (to him) is a dative prepositional clitic placed before the verb; گفت (said) is the reported-speech verb (here the past 'said') with شاه (the king) as subject. آنچ دانی is a relative phrase 'that which you know' (آنچ = 'that which', دانی = 2nd-person present 'you know'), and بگوی is the hortative/imperative form 'say/tell' addressing the addressee. Word order is topical (بدو) + reporting verb + subject, then the command (آنچ دانی بگوی).
segments.s385-g34: که introduces the subordinate clause giving purpose/result ('so that' / 'that'). هم ... و هم is the correlative 'both ... and ...'. راست‌گویی and راه‌جوی are abstract/nominal predicates ('truth-telling' and 'seeker of the way'); the copular verb ('be') is omitted and understood. The clause therefore expresses the wished-for state/result of the admonition.
segments.s385-g35: بدو 'to him' again marks the addressee of the speech. گفت is the reporting verb ('said') and جاماسپ is the speaker (here named). کای is a vocative particle 'O', and شهریار is the vocative 'king' addressed. Word order places the dative before the reporting verb and the vocative after the speaker's name.
segments.s385-g36: سخن (speech/word) is the object; بشنو is the 2nd-person singular imperative 'listen/hear'. از من 'from me' marks the source. یکی هوشیار is an appositive/vocative phrase: یکی 'a certain / one' and هوشیار an adjective 'wise' used here to address the king respectfully ('O wise one').
segments.s385-g37: تو is the emphatic 2nd-person pronoun 'you'; دانی is 2nd-person present 'you know'. که introduces the content clause 'that ...' and فرزندت اسفندیار is the subject of that clause (فرزندت = 'your son', with ت = possessive suffix; اسفندیار in apposition, the name).
segments.s385-g38: همی is a frequentative/intensifying particle common in classical Persian (here 'ever/constantly'). بند is 'bond/fetter' used as the state in which the son is. ساید is an archaic/formal verbal/adjectival form indicating being held/established in that state (here 'is/shall be bound'); به marks the oblique relation and بد روزگار 'ill fortune' gives the cause/agent ('by ill fate' / 'through ill fortune'). The whole means 'is continually bound by ill fortune.'
segments.s385-g39: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. شاه is the subject ('the king'); بگشاید is the subjunctive/conditional form of 'to open/loosen/release' used here ('releases/frees'). او را is the definite object 'him' (را marks definite object), and ز بند (ز = poetic form of از) means 'from the bond(s)'.
segments.s385-g40: نماند is the negative form (ن- + ماند) meaning 'will not/remain not' (3rd-person result of the conditional). برین is the prepositional/ demonstrative compound (بر + این) 'on/over this' and modifies کوهسار بلند 'the high/lofty mountain'. The clause states the conditional result: 'he will not remain on this lofty mountain.'
segments.s385-g73: This is a verb‑initial clause typical of older Persian: بپوشید is a past action ('he put on' / 'he donned') and the person-name جاماسپ follows as the subject. توزی قبای is a noun phrase (adjective/modifier + noun) describing the garment; the modifier precedes the noun here in classical word order. The verb prefix بـ here marks the preterite/finite action in Ferdowsi's style.
segments.s385-g74: فرود آمد is a compound verbal expression meaning 'descended / came down' (فرود 'descent' + آمد 'came'). از کوه is the source phrase 'from the mountain' (از = from + کوه = mountain). بی‌رهنمای is a postpositive adjective 'without a guide / unguided', formed with the prefix بی‑ plus رهنما.
segments.s385-g75: به سر بر نهاده is a classical locative + verbal phrase: به سر = 'on the head', بر = 'upon', نهاده is the past participle/preterite 'placed' (he had placed). کلاه دو پر is the noun phrase 'a hat with two feathers' (دو = two, پر = feather). The overall structure describes a completed action of arranging the headgear.
segments.s385-g76: برآیین ترکان is an ablative/adiaphoric phrase meaning 'according to the custom/manner of the Turks' (برآیین = 'according to custom' + ترکان = 'the Turks'). ببسته is the past/perfect participle 'had tied/fastened' and کمر is the object 'waist / belt' (here 'girdle' or 'girded waist'). The verb is placed before its object in this classical ordering.
segments.s385-g77: یکی is the indefinite 'a certain / one', followed by اسپ ترکی 'Turkish steed' (اسپ = horse, ترکی = Turkish). بیاورد (brought/has brought) is the past tense verb; پیش functions as 'before / in front (of them)'. The clause is verb-final in effect because the verb appears after the subject phrase in this poetic style.
segments.s385-g78: This is a descriptive clause: ابر اسپ is a poetic epithet for the steed ('a cloud‑like / great steed'), followed by آلت ز اندازه بیش which qualifies it as 'of size beyond measure' (ز اندازه = 'from/of measure' + بیش = 'more / beyond'). The line uses noun phrases and prepositional 'ز' to express an excess in size.
segments.s385-g79: نشست here carries the sense 'alighted / sat down' (past action). از بر باره is a classical locative/departure phrase meaning 'from upon the load/horse‑back' (بر باره = on the burden/back of the horse). و آمد به زیر coordinates a second action 'and came to below' — i.e. 'and came down to the ground' with به marking direction and زیر 'beneath / ground'.
segments.s385-g80: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause 'for / because'. بد مرد is a poetic characterization — بد here qualifies مرد as fierce/formidable rather than merely 'bad' in the moral sense; شایسته is a postpositive adjective 'worthy'. بر سان = 'in the manner of / like' and شیر = 'lion' gives the simile 'like a lion'. The whole clause explains why he acted as he did.
segments.s385-g17: همی is a habitual/continuous marker ('kept on' / 'would'). کشت here is the simple past of کشتن used in a habitual sense ('slew', 'kept slaying'). The phrase هر مهتری is 'every/each great man' (هر + noun), and بارگی (literally 'leaf/foliage') is used as an image/simile attached to مهتری ('each great man like a leaf').
segments.s385-g18: نهاند is a form of نهادن meaning 'puts/places' (here, 'he placed/caused'); دلها is the plural object 'hearts' (no را but definite by context); به + بیچارگی is a prepositional phrase 'into/unto misery' indicating the result/state. Word order is verb after its object phrase (poetic flexibility), yielding 'he placed hearts into misery.'
segments.s385-g19: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. لشکر is the subject 'the army/host'. چنان 'thus/so' modifies the action. گردشان (گرد + شان) means 'around them' (a circumstantial adverbial). برگرفت is a past tense compound verb 'took up/raised' used here of forming an encircling—'drew round/encircled.'
segments.s385-g20: کی is an interrogative 'who'. خوش‌منش is an adjectival epithet 'of good disposition / courteous' modifying the understood person. دست بر سر گرفت is an idiomatic verb phrase: دست گرفتن 'to take the hand' plus بر سر 'to the head' — a gesture of grief or dismay; گرفت is the past of گرفتن 'took/seized'. The whole segment asks rhetorically which courteous man made this gesture.
segments.s385-g21: جهاندیده is an epithet 'worldly‑wise' describing جاماسپ. جاماسپ را uses the object marker را to mark Jamasp as the one addressed. پیش خواند is a verb phrase 'called/summoned before (him)': پیش 'in the presence of / before' + خواند (here 'summoned/called'). The sequence means 'he summoned Jamasp to his presence.'
segments.s385-g22: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/about'. اختر فراوان is a noun phrase 'the abundant stars' (اختر 'star', فراوان 'numerous/abundant') functioning as the topic of what follows. سخنها is the plural 'words/talks/sayings'. براند here is a verbal form of راندن used transitively — with سخنها as object it means 'uttered/drove forth many words' (i.e. 'spoke much').
segments.s385-g23: بدو is the clitic combination 'to him' (he addressed him). گفت is the past tense 'he said'. کز is a fused relative/prepositional particle 'that/from which' — here 'from' (introducing the source/topic). گردش آسمان is a genitive phrase 'the motion/circuit of the sky/heavens' serving as the topic for what should be reported.
segments.s385-g24: بگوی is an imperative/subjunctive form of گفتن 'say/tell' (here a direct instruction). آنچ (آن‌چ) = 'that which' functions as the relative object: 'what you know'. دانی is present of دانستن 'you know'. و 'and' links the clauses. پنهان ممان is a prohibitive: پنهان 'hidden' + ممان (from ممانعت/ممانعت کردن 'to withhold/refuse') meaning 'do not withhold' — 'do not keep hidden anything.'
segments.s385-g65: کنون‌گر is a temporal + conditional combination (کنون “now” + -گر “if”), so the whole phrase sets an if-clause. بیایی is the present/subjunctive/optative form of آمدن (‘you come’/‘you should come’) used here as the protasis. دل is the patient/goal ‘heart’ and دل از کینه پاک places the ablative phrase از کینه (‘from hatred’) after it with the adjective پاک (‘clean/free’) following the noun: literally ‘make the heart clean of hatred.’
segments.s385-g66: This is the apodosis: the noun phrase سر دشمنان (‘the heads of the enemies’) is fronted as object. اندر آری is an archaic verbal phrase meaning ‘you would bring/put (into/on)’, where اندر = ‘into/on/among’ and آری here is an old verb-form (cf. آوردن) functioning as the verb. به خاک (‘to the earth/to the dust’) is a directional complement. In short: ‘you would put the enemies’ heads into the dust/earth.’
segments.s385-g67: وگرنه ‘otherwise/if not’ introduces the warning; شد is used here as a resultative/auxiliary (‘will be/shall be’ or ‘will come to pass’) and applies to the noun phrase این پادشاهی و تخت (‘this kingship and (its) throne’). Word order is subject after the auxiliary in this terse, poetic line.
segments.s385-g68: ز بن literally ‘from the root/foundation’ (poetic ز = از) gives the source. برکنند is a plural verb ‘they remove/uproot’ (present/subjunctive/optative form used for what will happen). این کیانی درخت (‘this Kayanian tree’) is a metaphorical object (the royal house/dynasty) placed after the verb by poetic inversion.
segments.s385-g69: چو آیی is a conditional/temporal introducer here meaning ‘if/when you come’ (چو used as ‘if’). سپارم is first-person singular (‘I entrust/I will entrust’); the enclitic ت (here separated in the line) marks the second-person object ‘to you’ and را is the object marker attached before the direct object, so the core means ‘I will entrust to you (ترا) تاج و گنج’ — ‘the crown and the treasure.’
segments.s385-g70: ز چیزی که (‘from that which/that which’) begins a relative clause explaining the source: من گرد کردم is ‘I gathered/collected’ (گرد کردن ‘to go about, gather’ + کردم ‘I did’), and به رنج is an adverbial phrase ‘with hardship/through toil’ describing how the speaker gathered it.
segments.s385-g71: بدین گفته = ‘by/with this saying’ (بدین = به + این). یزدان گوای منست is a compact clause: یزدان (‘God’) + گوای (‘witness’/‘attesting one’) + منست (an archaic copular/predicative formation here meaning ‘is to me’ → ‘is my witness’). The line uses older forms and compressed word order typical of epic diction.
segments.s385-g72: چو here means ‘as’ (introducing a comparison). جاماسپ is the name Jamasp, کو is an archaic relative/interrogative particle here functioning like ‘who/which’, and رهنمای منست again puts ‘guide’ (رهنمای) with the predicative منست to mean ‘is my guide.’ The phrase thus says ‘as Jamasp, who is my guide.’
segments.s385-g57: برو is an imperative, 2nd-person singular (“go”); what follows is a short command clause. وز here is the archaic, contracted sequence ו + از (and/from) used in classical Persian, introducing the source of the greeting. منش is a poetic/formal word functioning here as the source = “from me/my person” (classical diction). فراوان modifies درود, meaning “many/much” (an adverb/adjective intensifier). درود is the noun “greeting(s).”
segments.s385-g58: This is a small clause giving the manner/time of passage. شب تیره is a noun + adjective (“the dark night”). ناگاه is an adverb “suddenly/unexpectedly.” بگذر is an imperative or hortative form (the b- prefix is classical; here giving the command “pass/move on”). ز is the poetic preposition “from/by,” and رود is the noun “river.” The order is typical classical Persian: time/manner phrase before the verb, then the prepositional phrase.
segments.s385-g59: بگویش is the imperative بگو plus enclitic ش = “tell him.” که introduces the reported/subordinate clause (“that” / relative marker). آنکس که بیداد کرد is a noun + relative clause: آنکس (“that person/the one”) + که (“who/that”) + بیداد کرد (“committed injustice,” past tense). کرد is the past of ‘to do’ and governs بیداد.
segments.s385-g60: بشد is an archaic/polished past-form meaning roughly “became / was / came to be” and here conveys ‘has gone/has ceased to be’ (i.e. departed). زین is a contraction of از این (poetic “from this”); جهان here means “this life / the world.” با introduces an accompanying circumstance (“with”), دلی is a noun ‘a heart’ (here ‘a heart’), پر is the adjective/adverb “full (of),” and ز + درد = “of pain / of sorrow.” The whole phrase means “has gone from this world with a heart full of sorrow.”
segments.s385-g61: اگر introduces a conditional (“if”). من is the 1st-person pronoun. برفتم here is a classical form meaning “I go / I depart / I am gone” (past-form used in conditional sense in classical Persian). بگفت کسی is a verbless-future/habitual clause in which بگفت (present/habitual form of ‘say’) with کسی (“someone”) yields “someone will say” / “someone will say (that) ….”
segments.s385-g62: که again introduces the content of what will be said (“that”). بهره نبودش is the predicate “he had no share”: بهره = “share/portion,” نبودش = نبود + ش (negative past + 3rd-person clitic) = “was not his / he did not have.” ز دانش بسی is a classical ordering: ز = “of/from,” دانش = “learning/knowledge,” بسی is a postpositive intensifier meaning “much / enough” (here indicating large amount of learning that he did not have).
segments.s385-g63: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning “when.” بیداد کردم is the clause “I committed injustice” (بیداد = injustice; کردم = I did). بسیچم همی is classical diction: بسیچم is an emphatic/adverbial formation meaning “very / greatly” with a 1st-person flavour here, and همی is the habitual/continuative marker (classical particle often used with verbs), so together they convey a repeated/intense state (“I was greatly / I was much distressed”).
segments.s385-g64: وزان is an archaic fused phrase = و از آن (“and/from that [time/thing]”). کرده is the participle/past “having done” or “made,” linking to the following verb. خوی + ش forms خویش (here written split) meaning one’s disposition / one’s own nature (the ش is the clitic). پیچم is from پیچیدن “to turn/twist” with 1st-person ending (I turn/change), and همی again marks habitual/continuative aspect. The clause means roughly “and from that (having done it) I change/turn my disposition.”
segments.s385-g81: هرانکس که ... به راه is a relative clause: 'هرانکس که' = 'whoever (that)', with که introducing the clause. او را is the definite-object phrase (او + را) marking 'him' as the object of بدیدی. بدیدی is a past-tense verb 'saw' used here in the relative clause. به راه is an adverbial phrase 'on the road' (به + راه).
segments.s385-g82: بپرسیدی is the b- prefixed past form used here as a hortative/imperative 'ask' (classical Persian uses بـ + past to give a command or exhortation). او را is the object 'him', and ز introduces the matter about which one is to ask ('about/from' poetically). توران سپاه = 'the army of Turan' (توران modifies سپاه).
segments.s385-g83: به آواز ترکی is an adverbial phrase 'in the Turkish voice/intonation' with به + noun used to mark manner ('به' here = 'in' for language/voice). سخن راندی is a verbal phrase 'uttered speech / spoke' where راندی (from راندن) is used metaphorically with سخن to mean 'to speak/utter'. The clause is narrative/imperative depending on context (here it functions as an instruction).
segments.s385-g84: بگفتی (بـ + past) again functions as a hortative/imperative 'tell' or 'say to'. بد‌ان کس is 'to that man' (بدان = به + آن contracted; here parsed as ب + دان in the data), and که او خواندی is a relative clause 'whom you called/summoned' with خواندی as the past verb 'called/summoned'.
segments.s385-g85: ندانستی is the negative past 'did not know' (here functioning as 'no one knew'). Word order places او را (him, object) before the subject کسی; in this negative context کسی has the force 'no one'. حال و کار is a compound 'state and affairs' (two nouns joined by و).
segments.s385-g86: بگفتی is the hortative/imperative 'tell/say'. به ترکی سخن is the manner phrase 'in Turkish speech' (به = 'in' for language + ترکی 'Turkish' + سخن 'speech'). هوشیار here modifies the addressee as 'wise/alert' and serves as a vocative or injunction ('O wise one' / 'be alert').
segments.s385-g87: همی is a continuous/progressive particle 'kept on / continuously'. راند is the past 'drove' (here 'kept driving'); باره is the direct object 'load/burden'. به کردار باد is a simile/phrase 'in the manner of the wind' (کردار = manner/conduct, باد = 'wind'), describing how the load was driven.
segments.s385-g88: چنین تا introduces a temporal clause 'thus until...' with تا marking 'until'. بیامد is the past 'came' (narrative past). بر شاه زاد = 'upon the king's offspring/prince' (بر = on/upon; شاه زاد is a compound meaning 'born of the king' = prince).
segments.s385-g25: This subordinate clause begins with که introducing a purpose/nominal clause ('that/so that'); باشد is a subjunctive/optative form 'may be' (literary) pointing to a possibility. بدین بد is a small prepositional + demonstrative phrase 'in this evil / in this bad circumstance' (ب- 'in' + دین = demonstrative here), and مرا (written here as م + را) is the object/clitic meaning 'for me' (م = clitic related to 'me', را = object marker). دستگیر is a noun meaning 'helper, one who takes your hand' and completes the clause: 'that there may be for me a helper in this misfortune.'
segments.s385-g26: ببایدت is a literary fused expression meaning 'it is necessary for you / you must'; گفتن here is the infinitive 'to tell' or 'to say' used with the modal ببایدت; همه 'all' is the object, and ناگزیر is an adverb/adjectival complement 'compelled / unavoidable', reinforcing the necessity: 'you must tell all; it is unavoidable.' Word order places the modal before the infinitive, as in older Persian.
segments.s385-g27: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; بشنید is the past tense 'heard' (3rd sg.); جاماسپ is the subject. بر پای خاست is a light-verb construction meaning 'rose to his feet / stood up' (بر = on, پای = foot, خاست = rose). The clause is a simple temporal clause: 'When Jamasp heard, he stood up.'
segments.s385-g28: بدو is the dative clitic phrase 'to him'; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here glossed as 'he said to him'). کای is a vocative 'O', followed by خسرو 'Khosrow' (addressee). داد و راست are two nouns/qualities 'justice and truth / just and true' (predicate adjectives with an implicit copula), so the phrase reads as an address: 'O Khosrow, (you are) just and true.'
segments.s385-g29: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'; شاه is the subject 'the king'; گفتار من is the noun phrase 'my speech / my words' (گفتار = speech, من = my/I), and بشنود is the subjunctive/potential present 'he hears / should hear' used for the protasis of the conditional. The clause thus reads 'If the king hears my speech...'.
segments.s385-g30: بدین گردش is a prepositional phrase 'to/into this turning/course' (ب- 'in/to' + دین = demonstrative 'this' + گردش 'circulation, course'); اختران is the subject 'the stars'; بگرود is a literary verb meaning 'will turn / will (come to) adopt' (subjunctive/indicative sense in prophecy). The verb is at the end, as expected in Persian: 'the stars will turn to this course.'
segments.s385-g31: بگویم is a 1st-person present/subjunctive/future-like form 'I will tell / I shall say' (literary use). بدو 'to him' is the indirect object; هرچ دانم is a free relative 'whatever I know' (هرچ = whatever, دانم = I know); درست qualifies the manner 'truly / correctly.' Together: 'I will tell him whatever I know truly.'
segments.s385-g32: ز من 'from me' (ز = from, poetic variant of از); راستی جوی is an imperative/infinitival phrase 'seek the truth' (راستی = truth/honesty, جوی = seek/search); شاها is the vocative 'O king' inserted to address the king directly; نخست 'first' is an adverb setting the order. Word order here places the source first and the exhortation and address after: 'First, O king, seek truth from me.'

sentences.s385-s1: At last Gushtasp turned his back; thereupon his fortunes became harsh.
sentences.s385-s2: Then they rushed on for two stages; they contrived to seize him for me.
sentences.s385-s3: A certain mountain named Pargiyā came up before him; within it there was a spring and a mill.
sentences.s385-s4: For around that mountain there was one path, and Gushtasp knew that path.
sentences.s385-s25: If I see him on that battlefield, I will give him this crown, throne, and helmet.
sentences.s385-s26: That (is), to be made a companion in the presence of that esteemed one; he will free Maran the stranger from his bonds.
sentences.s385-s27: Jamasp said to him, 'O king, I am (one) who will go; this speech is not contemptible.'
sentences.s385-s28: The king Jahāndār said to Jamasp, 'May wisdom always be paired with you.'
sentences.s385-s45: When the wise one came into the plain, the dark night passed away from the host.
sentences.s385-s46: When he came near the fortress of Gonbadān, he freed himself from their hands.
sentences.s385-s21: Gushtasp said to him, "O truthful one, I desire nothing but the truth."
sentences.s385-s22: He said to Jamasp, "O wise man, because of that affair my heart was full of pain."
sentences.s385-s23: that I confined him in that banquet-hall by the words of an ill-wisher, and he is innocent.
sentences.s385-s24: At once I became remorseful because of that; my heart weary, I turned toward a cure.
sentences.s385-s5: King Gushtasp and his whole army went from the battlefield toward the mountain.
sentences.s385-s6: When Arjasp arrived there with his host, he looked about and saw no path up the mountain.
sentences.s385-s7: They hemmed him in on every side; when the magnanimous king became helpless ...
sentences.s385-s8: They kindled a fire on that mountainside; on those stones, thorn upon thorn, they burned.
sentences.s385-s17: The king said to him, "Tell what you know — so that you may be both truthful and a seeker of the right path."
sentences.s385-s18: Jamasp said to him, "O king, hear a word from me, O wise one."
sentences.s385-s19: You know that your son Esfandiyar is ever bound by ill fortune.
sentences.s385-s20: If the king frees him from his bonds, he will not remain on this high mountain.
sentences.s385-s37: Jamasp donned the tozi robe and came down from the mountain without a guide.
sentences.s385-s38: He had placed on his head a two‑feathered hat and girded his waist in the manner of the Turks.
sentences.s385-s39: Someone brought forward a Turkish steed — a great horse, its size beyond measure.
sentences.s385-s40: He dismounted from the pack and came down to the ground, for he was a formidable, worthy man like a lion.
sentences.s385-s9: He kept slaying every great man like leaves; he put hearts into misery.
sentences.s385-s10: When the host thus drew a ring about them, which courteous man lifted his hand to his head?
sentences.s385-s11: He summoned the worldly-wise Jamasp before him, and spoke plentifully of the stars.
sentences.s385-s12: He said to him: 'From the movement of the heavens tell what you know, and do not keep hidden (anything).'
sentences.s385-s33: If now you would come and cleanse your heart of hatred, you would lay the heads of your enemies upon the earth.
sentences.s385-s34: If not, this kingship and throne will be removed from their root — they will uproot this Kayanian tree.
sentences.s385-s35: If you come, I will entrust to you the crown and the treasure — what I have gathered with toil.
sentences.s385-s36: By this saying God is my witness, as Jamasp, who is my guide.
sentences.s385-s29: Go, and give him many greetings from me; pass suddenly through the dark night by the river.
sentences.s385-s30: Tell him that the one who committed injustice has departed this world with a heart full of sorrow.
sentences.s385-s31: If I go (am gone), someone will say that he had little share of learning.
sentences.s385-s32: When I did injustice I was greatly (distressed), and from that having done it I turn my nature.
sentences.s385-s41: Whoever saw him on the road, ask him about the army of Turan.
sentences.s385-s42: Speak in the Turkish voice; tell that man whom you called.
sentences.s385-s43: No one knew his state and affairs; say (it) in Turkish speech, O sensible one.
sentences.s385-s44: It kept driving the load like the wind, thus until it came upon the king's son.
sentences.s385-s13: That in this ill (turn) there might be a helper for me; you must tell (it) all — you are compelled to do so.
sentences.s385-s14: When Jamasp heard, he rose to his feet and said to him, 'O Khosrow, (you are) just and true.'
sentences.s385-s15: If the king should hear my words, the stars will (or will be made to) turn to this course.
sentences.s385-s16: I will tell him whatever I know correctly; first, O king, seek the truth from me.
